instruction:
The Question Paper contains three sections.
 Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions.
 All questions carry equal marks.
 There is no negative marking.

questions.question: Generally, the first ionization energy increases along a period including some exceptions. One which is NOT an exception is ______.
questions.question_html: Generally, the first ionization energy increases along a period including some exceptions. One which is NOT an exception is ______.
questions.explanation: The high ionization energy of B, N, and Mg is due to the presence of stable electronic configuration in these atoms as compared to the next element in the period. For example, Be (Is^(2) 2s^(2)) has completely filled orbitals as compared to B (1s^(2) 2s^(2) 2p^(1)) where there is one electron in p-orbital that can easily be lost. Therefore, B has lower ionization energy than Be.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the electron is incorrect?
questions.question_html: Which of the following statements about the electron is incorrect?

options.text: It is a basic constituent of all atoms.
options.html: It is a basic constituent of all atoms.

options.text: It is a constituent of cathode rays.
options.html: It is a constituent of cathode rays.

options.text: It is a negatively charged particle.
options.html: It is a negatively charged particle.

options.text: The mass of electron is equal to the mass of neutron.
options.html: The mass of electron is equal to the mass of neutron.

correct_options: The mass of electron is equal to the mass of neutron.
questions.explanation:
Mass of one electron= 9.1 $\times$ 10^(-31) kg

Mass of neutron =1.67 $\times$ 10^(-27) kg. Therefore, they are not equal.

questions.question: The increasing order of bond dissociation energies of the following species is_____.
questions.question_html: The increasing order of bond dissociation energies of the following species is_____.
questions.explanation:
As bond order increases bond dissociation energy also increases.

Thus N_(2)

 The triple bonded N_(2) with bond order = 3 has the highest bond dissociation energy.
 The doubly bonded O_(2) with bond order = 2 has lesser bond dissociation energy than N_(2)
 F_(2) and Cl_(2) both are single bonded having the same bond order = 1 

But, the electron-electron repulsive force (e-e repulsion) is maximum in the F_(2) molecule due to its small size. This accounts for the lower bond dissociation energy of F_(2 ) as compared to Cl_(2).

questions.question: The number of dots around the Lewis symbols for the elements represent:
questions.question_html: The number of dots around the Lewis symbols for the elements represent:

options.text: the number of valence electrons of the element
options.html: the number of valence electrons of the element

correct_options: the number of valence electrons of the element
questions.explanation:
Gilbert N. Lewis is widely known for his use of simple symbolic representations of elements that show valence electrons as dots.

The Lewis electron-dot symbols focus on the electrons in the highest principal energy level in the atom, the valence electrons.

After all, these are the electrons that participate in chemical reactions. Lewis electron-dot symbols work well for the representative elements.

questions.question: Which of the following is most powerful oxidizing agent in the following:
questions.question_html: Which of the following is most powerful oxidizing agent in the following:
questions.explanation: Ozone act as powerful oxidising agent because it can easily decompose to give an atom of nascent oxygen which is more reactive than O_(2).

questions.question: Ammonia (NH_(3)) is an example of:
questions.question_html: Ammonia (NH3) is an example of:
questions.explanation: Dihydrogen under certain reaction conditions combines with almost all elements except noble gases to form compounds called hydrides. Electron –rich hydrides, as the name suggests, are compounds of hydrogen with other elements that have excess electrons present as lone-pairs. For example, ammonia –NH_(3) is a hydride of nitrogen that has one lone pair, water (H_(2)O) is a hydride of oxygen that has two lone pairs, etc. As these are electron-rich species, they can donate electrons that is they are lewis bases.

questions.question: Electronegativity of carbon atoms depends upon their state of hybridisation. In which of the following compounds, the carbon marked with asterisk is most electronegative?
questions.question_html: Electronegativity of carbon atoms depends upon their state of hybridisation. In which of the following compounds, the carbon marked with asterisk is most electronegative?
questions.explanation: Greater is the s character of the hybridised orbital, greater is the electronegativity of the atom. In a triply bonded C, there is sp hybridisation. In sp hybridization, the atom has maximum s character (50%). So in this case the carbon with a triple bond has maximum electronegativity.

questions.question: For the reaction, 2 N_(2)H_(4) (l) + N_(2)O_(4 )(l) $\to $ 3N_(2)(g) + 4H_(2)O(l), if 160 g of N_(2)H_(4 )is mixed with 160 g N_(2)O_(4), the yield of H_(2)O is:
questions.question_html: For the reaction, 2 N2H4 (l) + N2O4 (l) {tex}\to {/tex} 3N2(g) + 4H2O(l), if 160 g of N2H4 is mixed with 160 g N2O4, the yield of H2O is:
questions.explanation:
Calculations:

Moles of N_(2)H_(4)

= $\frac{160}{32}$ 

= 5 moles 

Moles of N_(2)O_(4)

= $\frac{160}{92}$ 

= 1.739 moles

As per stoichiometry of the given reaction,

2 moles of N_(2)H_(4) require 1 mole of N_(2)O_(4),

 $\therefore$ 5 mole of N_(2)H_(4) will need 2.5 moles of N_(2)O_(4)

. But we have only 1.73 mole of N_(2)O_(4) so it would be consumed first.

Thus N_(2)O_(4) is the limiting reagent for this reaction.

Again, since 1 mole of N_(2)O_(4 )yields water

= 4 mole of water

 $ \therefore$ 1.73 mole of N_(2)O_(4) will yield water

= 6.92 moles

 $\equiv $ (6.92 $\times$ 18) g

= 124.56g of water

questions.question: The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of the hydrogen atom is about −3.4 eV. What is the kinetic energy of the electron in this state?
questions.question_html: The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of the hydrogen atom is about −3.4 eV. What is the kinetic energy of the electron in this state?
questions.explanation:
Total energy of the electron, E = −3.4 eV

Kinetic energy of the electron is equal to the negative of the total energy.

K = −E = − (− 3.4) = +3.4 eV

Hence, the kinetic energy of the electron in the given state is +3.4 eV.

questions.question: The elements present in an organic compound are converted from covalent form into the ionic form by fusing the compound with sodium metal. This test is called:
questions.question_html: The elements present in an organic compound are converted from covalent form into the ionic form by fusing the compound with sodium metal. This test is called:
questions.explanation: Nitrogen, sulphur, halogens, and phosphorus present in an organic compound are detected by “Lassaigne’s test”. The elements present in the compound are converted from the covalent form into the ionic form by fusing the compound with sodium metal. Cyanide, sulphide and halide of sodium so formed on sodium fusion are extracted from the fused mass by boiling it with distilled water. This extract is known as sodium fusion extract.

questions.question: 788 kJ of energy is required to separate one mole of solid NaCl into one mole of Na^(+) (g) and one mole of Cl^(-) (g) to an infinite distance. This process explains:
questions.question_html: 788 kJ of energy is required to separate one mole of solid NaCl into one mole of Na+ (g) and one mole of Cl- (g) to an infinite distance. This process explains:
questions.explanation: Lattice enthalpy is simply the change in Enthalpy associated with the formation of one mole of an ionic compound from its oppositely charged ions in their standard states under standard conditions.

questions.question:
Which of the following are not redox reactions?

a) K_(4)[Fe(CN)_(6)] + H_(2)SO_(4) + H_(2)O $ \to $ K_(2)SO_(4) + CO + FeSO_(4) + (NH_(4))_(2)SO_(4).

b) $CuS{O_4} + N{H_3} \to [Cu{(N{H_3})_4}S{O_4}$ .

c)​​​​​​​ $Mg + {N_2} \to M{g_3}{N_2}$ .

d) ${I_2} + 3C{l_2} \to 2IC{l_3}$
questions.question_html:
Which of the following are not redox reactions?

a) K4[Fe(CN)6] + H2SO4 + H2O {tex} \to {/tex} K2SO4 + CO + FeSO4 + (NH4)2SO4.

b)  {tex}CuS{O_4} + N{H_3} \to [Cu{(N{H_3})_4}S{O_4}{/tex}.

c)​​​​​​​ {tex}Mg + {N_2} \to M{g_3}{N_2}{/tex}.

d) {tex}{I_2} + 3C{l_2} \to 2IC{l_3}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
a) K_(4)[Fe(CN)_(6)] + H_(2)SO_(4) + H_(2)O $ \to $ K_(2)SO_(4) + CO + FeSO_(4) + (NH_(4))_(2)SO_(4)

b) $CuS{O_4} + N{H_3} \to [Cu{(N{H_3})_4}S{O_4}$ 

Since, oxidation number of each element does not change in these reactions, so these are not redox reactions.

questions.question: Which is used hydrogen generators:
questions.question_html: Which is used hydrogen generators:
questions.explanation:
NaH

 $N a H + H _ { 2 } O \rightarrow N a O H + H _ { 2 } \uparrow$

questions.question: Delocalisation of σ-electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p-orbital is called
questions.question_html: Delocalisation of σ-electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p-orbital is called
questions.explanation: The interaction of the electrons in a sigma bond (usually C–H or C–C) with an adjacent empty (or partially filled) non-bonding p-orbital, antibonding $\sigma$ or $\pi$ orbital or filled $\pi$ orbital, to give an extended molecular orbital that increases the stability of the system is Hyperconjugation.

questions.question: In a homogeneous mixture, the components are:
questions.question_html: In a homogeneous mixture, the components are:

options.text: do not mix with each other and its composition is uniform throughout.
options.html: do not mix with each other and its composition is uniform throughout.

options.text: do not mix with each other and its composition is not uniform throughout.
options.html: do not mix with each other and its composition is not uniform throughout.

options.text: completely mixed with each other and its composition is uniform throughout.
options.html: completely mixed with each other and its composition is  uniform throughout.

options.text: completely mix with each other and its composition is not uniform throughout.
options.html: completely mix with each other and its composition is not uniform throughout.

correct_options: completely mixed with each other and its composition is uniform throughout.
questions.explanation:
In a homogeneous mixture,

 the components are mixed together completely.
 has a uniform composition throughout.

questions.question: The number of radial nodes for 3p orbital is __________.
questions.question_html: The number of radial nodes for 3p orbital is __________.
questions.explanation:
Number of radial nodes = n−l−1

where n = principal quantum number, l = azimuthal quantum number

For 3p orbital, n=3 and l=1

 $\therefore$ The number of radial nodes for 3p orbital =3−1−1=3−2=1.

questions.question: Electrochemical processes for extraction of highly reactive metal and non-metal involves one of the following. Choose the most appropriate option.
questions.question_html: Electrochemical processes for extraction of highly reactive metal and non-metal involves one of the following. Choose the most appropriate option.
questions.explanation: The electrochemical process for extraction of metal or non-metal from its ore involves the redox reaction i.e. oxidation and reduction takes place simultaneously. For example, extraction of Na and Cl_(2 )from NaCl.

questions.question: The inertness of noble gas was observed to be due to their electronic configurations. Choose the most appropriate:
questions.question_html: The inertness of noble gas was observed to be due to their electronic configurations. Choose the most appropriate:

options.text: Atomic mass is low so they are stable.
options.html: Atomic mass is low so they are stable.

options.text: Noble gases form ions to complete their outermost orbitals.
options.html: Noble gases form ions to complete their outermost orbitals.

options.text: Total number of electrons in the outermost shell is duplets.
options.html: Total number of electrons in the outermost shell is duplets.

options.text: Outermost orbitals of the noble gases are fully filled.
options.html: Outermost orbitals of the noble gases are fully filled.

correct_options: Outermost orbitals of the noble gases are fully filled.
questions.explanation: The six noble gases (Helium, Neon, Argon, Krypton, Xenon, Radon) are found in group 18 of the periodic table. These elements were considered to be inert gases until the 1960s because their oxidation number of 0 prevents the noble gases from forming compounds readily. All noble gases have the maximum number of electrons possible in their outer shell (2 for Helium, 8 for all others), making them stable.

questions.question: Carius tube is used in the estimations of which one of the following?
questions.question_html: Carius tube is used in the estimations of which one of the following?
questions.explanation: It is used in the estimation of Halogen and Sulphur. For estimation of sulphur a known mass of an organic compound is heated in a hard glass tube called a Carius tube with sodium peroxide or fuming nitric acid. For estimation of halogen, a known mass of an organic compound is heated with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate contained in a Carius tube, in a furnace.

questions.question: Choose the most appropriate option to complete the statement - " Whenever there is a rearrangement of atoms which makes or breaks chemical bonds __________."
questions.question_html: Choose the most appropriate option to complete the statement - " Whenever there is a rearrangement of atoms which makes or breaks chemical bonds __________."
questions.explanation: The occurrence of a chemical reaction is always associated with the breaking and formation of bonds of the reactant and products along with rearrangement of their atoms, hence the statement at option 1 is most appropriate i.e. " Whenever there is a rearrangement of atoms which makes or breaks chemical bonds a chemical reaction takes place."

questions.question: 15 km is equal to ______ m?
questions.question_html: 15 km is equal to ______ m?
questions.explanation:
Since, 1 km

= 1000 m

 $\therefore$ 15 km 

= ( 15 $\times$ 1000 ) m

= 15000 m.

questions.question: Silver article turns black when kept in the open for a few days due to formation of
questions.question_html: Silver article turns black when kept in the open for a few days due to formation of
questions.explanation: Ag_(2)S

questions.question: The formation of the Cl_(2 )molecule can be understood in terms of the sharing of a pair of electrons between the two chlorine atoms, each chlorine atom contributing one electron to the shared pair. Choose the most appropriate name of the bond that is formed:
questions.question_html: The formation of the Cl2 molecule can be understood in terms of the sharing of a pair of electrons between the two chlorine atoms, each chlorine atom contributing one electron to the shared pair. Choose the most appropriate name of the bond that is formed:
questions.explanation: Two chlorine atoms will each share one electron to get a full outer shell and form a stable Cl_(2) molecule. By sharing the two electrons where the shells touch each chlorine atom can count 8 electrons in its outer shell. These full outer shells with their shared electrons are now stable and the Cl_(2) molecule will not react further with other chlorine atoms. One pair of shared electrons form a single covalent bond.

questions.question: Covalent bonds are formed with other carbon atoms due to:
questions.question_html: Covalent bonds are formed with other carbon atoms due to:
questions.explanation: Catenation is the linkage of atoms of the same element into longer chains. Catenation occurs most readily in carbon, which forms covalent bonds with other carbon atoms to form longer chains and structures. This is the reason for the presence of the vast number of organic compounds in nature.

questions.question: Which of the following reactions is not correct according to the law of conservation of mass.
questions.question_html: Which of the following reactions is not correct according to the law of conservation of mass.
questions.explanation:
According to law of conservation of mass, the number of atoms in each side of reaction must be equal. i.e mass of reactant = mass of product.

In this equation, No. of atoms in the reactant side is not equal to the no. of atoms in the product side.

Hence, law of conservation of mass is not satisfied.

questions.question: The wave character of electrons is used in the construction of
questions.question_html: The wave character of electrons is used in the construction of
questions.explanation: The electron microscope uses a beam of accelerated electrons as a source of illumination. The wavelength of an electron being much shorter than the visible light the electron microscope has a higher resolving power.

questions.question: Using MO theory predicts which of the following species has the shortest bond length?
questions.question_html: Using MO theory predicts which of the following species has the shortest bond length?
questions.explanation: Bond length is inversely proportional to bond order. $O_2^{2 + }$ has highest bond order as electrons are removed from antibonding MO.

questions.question: The ionic hydrides are crystalline, non-volatile but on melting does one of the following:
questions.question_html: The ionic hydrides are crystalline, non-volatile but on melting does one of the following:
questions.explanation: They are formed when hydrogen molecule reacts with most s-block elements which are highly electropositive in nature. In solid-state, the ionic hydrides are crystalline, non-conducting, and non-volatile. However, in a liquid state, it conducts electricity. Ionic hydrides on electrolysis liberate hydrogen gas at the anode.

questions.question: Polarisation of $\sigma$ -bond caused by the polarisation of adjacent $\sigma$ -bond is termed as:
questions.question_html: Polarisation of {tex}\sigma{/tex}-bond caused by the polarisation of adjacent {tex}\sigma{/tex}-bond is termed as:
questions.explanation: Polarisation of $\sigma$ -bond caused by the polarisation of the adjacent $\sigma$ -bond is referred to as the inductive effect. This effect is passed on to the subsequent bonds also but the effect decreases rapidly as the number of intervening bonds increases and becomes vanishingly small after three bonds. The inductive effect is related to the ability of substituent(s) to either withdraw or donate electron density to the attached carbon atom.

questions.question: In the organic compound CH_(2)=CH–CH_(2)–CH_(2)–C $ \equiv $ CH, the pair of hydridised orbitals involved in the formation of C_(2)–C_(3) bond is:
questions.question_html: In the organic compound CH2=CH–CH2–CH2–C{tex} \equiv {/tex}CH, the pair of hydridised orbitals involved in the formation of C2–C3 bond is:
questions.explanation:
C_(2)-C 

 $(C{H_2} = \mathop {CH}\limits^{(S{P^2})} - \mathop {C{H_2}}\limits^{(S{P^3})} - C{H_2} - C \equiv CH)$

questions.question: Mg^(2+) is isoelectronic with:
questions.question_html: Mg2+ is isoelectronic with:
questions.explanation:
Isoelectronic species refers to the elements that have the same number of electrons.

Mg^(2+) is 10 electron species $(1s^2, 2s^2, 2p^6)$ . Its configuration is like that of Ne.

Thus it is isoelectronic with any element having 10e^(-) or we can say 8e^(- )in its valence shell.

Atomic number of Sodium (Na) is 11 after loosing one electron it became Na^(+) and have 10 electrons.

Thus Mg^(2+) is isoelectronic with Na^(+).

questions.question: Which of the followings options represents the correct bond order?
questions.question_html: Which of the followings options represents the correct bond order?
questions.explanation:
N_(b) electron > N_(a) electrons and also depends on bond order.

B.O. = $\frac{1}{2}$ (N_(b) - N_(a))

B.O of O_(2) =2, O

questions.question: Dihydrogen can be prepared on a commercial scale by different methods. In its preparation by the action of steam on hydrocarbons, a mixture of CO and H_(2) gas is formed. It is known as ________.
questions.question_html: Dihydrogen can be prepared on a commercial scale by different methods. In its preparation by the action of steam on hydrocarbons, a mixture of CO and H2 gas is formed. It is known as ________.
questions.explanation: Water gas is a combustion fuel containing carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen gas (H_(2)). Water-gas is made by passing steam overheated hydrocarbons. The water-gas shift reaction can be used to reduce carbon dioxide levels and enrich hydrogen content, making water gas.

questions.question: Inorganic compounds, when homolytic cleavage takes place, one of the electrons of the shared pair in a covalent bond goes with each of the bonded atoms. This results in the formation of:
questions.question_html: Inorganic compounds, when homolytic cleavage takes place, one of the electrons of the shared pair in a covalent bond goes with each of the bonded atoms. This results in the formation of:
questions.explanation: In homolytic cleavage or homolysis, the two electrons in a cleaved covalent bond are divided equally between the products. Thus, in homolytic cleavage, the movement of a single electron takes place instead of an electron pair. This process is also known as homolytic fission. Such cleavage results in the formation of neutral species (atom or group) which contains an unpaired electron. These species are called free radicals.

questions.question: The number of grams of oxygen in 0.10 mol of Na_(2)CO_(3). 10H_(2)O is
questions.question_html: The number of grams of oxygen in 0.10 mol of Na2CO3. 10H2O is
questions.explanation:
Calculations:

1 mole of Na_(2)CO_(3).10H_(2)O contains 13 moles of oxygen atoms. 

0.1 mole Na_(2)CO_(3).10H_(2)O contains 13 × 0.1 = 1.3 moles of oxygen atoms. 

Mass of oxygen atoms = 1.3 × 16 = 20.8 g.

 $\therefore$ 0.10 mole of Na_(2 )CO_(3). 10 H_(2 )O would contain a number of grams of oxygen atoms = 20.8 g

questions.question: Charge on the electron was experimentally determined by:
questions.question_html: Charge on the electron was experimentally determined by:

options.text: Electrical discharge carried out in the modified cathode ray tube
options.html: Electrical discharge carried out in the modified cathode ray tube
R.A. Millikan's oil drop experiment

questions.explanation: In this experiment, a spray of oil droplets produced by an atomizer is allowed to fall in between two oppositely charged plates. These atoms are converted into ions with the help of X-rays. The oil droplets may absorb some of the electrons thus produced and acquire charge. The velocity of the charged oil droplet is measured as it falls freely under the influence of gravity and then in an electric field. From the values of velocity measured, it is possible to calculate the charge on the droplet which is considered to be the charge of the electron.

questions.question: Write the state of hybridisation of carbon in the compound, $HC \equiv N$ and shape of the molecule.
questions.question_html: Write the state of hybridisation of carbon in the compound, {tex}HC \equiv N{/tex} and shape of the molecule.
questions.explanation: As the C is bonded to N by a triple bond so it is sp hybridized andand the molecule is linear in shape.

questions.question: Considering x-axis as the internuclear axis which out of the following will not from a sigma bond and why?
questions.question_html: Considering x-axis as the internuclear axis which out of the following will not from a sigma bond and why?
questions.explanation:
For p-orbitals (l = 1), there are three possible orientations corresponding to m = -1, 0, +1 values. This means that there are three p - orbitals in each p-subshell. These are designated as p_(x), p_(y) and p_(z);

These three orbitals are equal in energy but differ in their orientations. Each orbital consists of two lobes symmetrical about a particular axis. Depending upon the orientation of the lobes, these are designated as 2p_(x), 2p_(y) and 2p_(z), as they are symmetrical about x, y and z-axes respectively. That is, 2p_(x) orbital has two lobes symmetrical around x-axis and the lobes of 2p_(z) orbital are symmetrical around z-axis while 2p_(y) orbital has two lobes symmetrical around y-axix so it doesn't form sigma bond.

questions.question:
Electrophiles are electron seeking species. Which of the following groups contain only electrophile

 ${\text{B}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{3}}},{\text{ N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}},{\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}$ 
 $AlCl_3, SO_3,NO_2^ + $ 
 ${\text{NO}}_2^ + ,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}^ + ,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}} - \mathop C\limits^ + = {\text{O}}$ 
 ${{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{H}}_5^ + , CH_3^+, NH_2^-$
questions.question_html:
Electrophiles are electron seeking species. Which of the following groups contain only electrophile

  1. {tex}{\text{B}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{3}}},{\text{ N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}},{\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}AlCl_3, SO_3,NO_2^ + {/tex}
  3. {tex}{\text{NO}}_2^ + ,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}^ + ,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}} - \mathop C\limits^ + = {\text{O}}{/tex}
  4. {tex}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{H}}_5^ + , CH_3^+, NH_2^-{/tex}
questions.explanation: Electrophile are electron loving species which can be positively charged ions or neutral molecules which are electron deficient. (ii) and (iii) contains all the electrophiles.

questions.question: Oil drop experiment is for determining the:
questions.question_html: Oil drop experiment is for determining the:
questions.explanation: The oil drop experiment was performed by Robert A. Millikan and Harvey Fletcher in 1909 to measure the elementary electric charge (the charge of the electron). The experiment entailed observing tiny electrically charged droplets of oil located between two parallel metal surfaces, forming the plates of a capacitor.

questions.question: In general, in the molecules containing multiple bonds, ________ provide the most reactive centres. Choose the most appropriate one.
questions.question_html: In general, in the molecules containing multiple bonds, ________ provide the most reactive centres. Choose the most appropriate one.
questions.explanation: $\pi$ bond formed by sideways overlapping of p orbitals/d orbitals on adjacent atoms provides the most reactive centers.

questions.question: Isobars are atoms having the

options.html: same mass number but different atomic number

options.html: same atomic number but different mass number

options.text: same atomic number but different number of neutrons
options.html: same atomic number but different number of neutrons

correct_options: same mass number but different atomic number
questions.explanation: Isobars are atoms (nuclides) of different chemical elements that have the same number of nucleons. Correspondingly, isobars differ in atomic number (or the number of protons) but have the same mass number.

questions.question: Isotopes are the atoms with
questions.question_html: Isotopes are the atoms with

options.text: same atomic number but different mass number
options.html: same atomic number but different mass number

options.text: same mass number but different atomic number
options.html: same mass number but different atomic number

options.text: same number of neutrons but different mass number
options.html: same number of neutrons but different mass number

options.text: same atomic number but different number of electrons
options.html: same atomic number but different number of electrons

correct_options: same atomic number but different mass number
questions.explanation: The atoms of a chemical element can exist in different types. These are called isotopes. They have the same number of protons (and electrons), but different numbers of neutrons. Different isotopes of the same element have different masses.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Line emission spectra help in the study of electronic configuration.

Reason (R): Each element has a unique line emission spectrum.
questions.question_html:
Assertion (A): Line emission spectra help in the study of electronic configuration.
Reason (R): Each element has a unique line emission spectrum.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

correct_options: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation: Line emission spectra gives information about electronic configuration which is unique to element by virtue of having that type of electronic arrangement.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): O^(2-), F^(-), Na^(+) and Mg^(2+) have the same number of electrons (10) and same atomic radius.

Reason (R): Some atoms and ions which contain the same number of electrons, are called isoelectronic species.
questions.question_html:
Assertion (A): O2-, F-, Na+ and Mg2+ have the same number of electrons (10) and same atomic radius.
Reason (R): Some atoms and ions which contain the same number of electrons, are called isoelectronic species.

questions.explanation: O^(2-), F^(-), Na^(+ )and Mg^(2+) have the same number of electrons (10). Their radii would be different because of their different nuclear charges.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Though the central atom of both NH_(3) and H_(2)O molecules are sp^(3) hybridized, yet H–N–H bond angle is greater than that of H–O–H.

Reason (R): This is because the nitrogen atom has one lone pair and the oxygen atom has two lone pairs.

Significant figures for 0.200 = 3

Significant figure for 200 = 1

Zeros at the end of a number without decimal point, may or may not be significant depending on the accuracy of the measurement.

questions.question: Molecular mass of a compound is the ________ of atomic masses of elements present in a molecule.
questions.question_html: Molecular mass of a compound is the ________ of atomic masses of elements present in a molecule.
questions.explanation:
Molecular mass is the sum of atomic masses of the elements present in a molecule.

For example ; Molecular mass of CO_(2 )= $\Sigma$ atomic mass of one carbon atom & atomic mass of two oxygen atoms

= [ 12 + 2(16) ]u

= 44u.

questions.question: Radio frequency region of the electromagnetic spectrum is used for broadcasting. It is
questions.question_html: Radio frequency region of the electromagnetic spectrum is used for broadcasting. It is
questions.explanation: Radiofrequency (RF) is any of the electromagnetic wave frequencies that lie in the range extending from around 3 kHz to 300 GHz, which include those frequencies used in radio communication or radar. RF usually refers to electrical rather than mechanical oscillations.

questions.question: Aniline is separated from aniline-water mixture using following technique:
questions.question_html: Aniline is separated from aniline-water mixture using following technique:
questions.explanation: Steam distillation is a special type of distillation (a separation process) for temperature-sensitive materials like natural aromatic compounds. It was a popular laboratory method for purification of organic compounds.

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
A quantitative measure of the tendency of an element to lose electrons is given by its Ionization Enthalpy. It represents the energy required to remove an electron from an isolated gaseous atom (X) in its ground state. The ionization enthalpy is expressed in units of kJ mol^(-1). We can define the second ionization enthalpy as the energy required to remove the second most loosely bound electron. Energy is always required to remove electrons from an atom and hence ionization enthalpies are always positive. The second ionization enthalpy will be higher than the first ionization enthalpy.

 ![image](https://media-mycbseguide.s3.amazonaws.com/images/imgur/1632228324-zzkbgx.jpg) 

Variation of first ionization enthalpies ( $\Delta_i$ H) with atomic number for elements with Z = 1 to 60. On the other hand, minima occur at the alkali metals and their low ionization enthalpies can be correlated with their high reactivity. In addition, you will notice two trends the first ionization enthalpy generally increases as we go across a period and decreases as we descend in a group. The ionization enthalpy and atomic radius are closely related properties.

The second ionization enthalpy will be higher than the first ionization enthalpy because
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:

A quantitative measure of the tendency of an element to lose electrons is given by its Ionization Enthalpy. It represents the energy required to remove an electron from an isolated gaseous atom (X) in its ground state. The ionization enthalpy is expressed in units of kJ mol-1. We can define the second ionization enthalpy as the energy required to remove the second most loosely bound electron. Energy is always required to remove electrons from an atom and hence ionization enthalpies are always positive. The second ionization enthalpy will be higher than the first ionization enthalpy.

Variation of first ionization enthalpies ({tex}\Delta_i{/tex}H) with atomic number for elements with Z = 1 to 60. On the other hand, minima occur at the alkali metals and their low ionization enthalpies can be correlated with their high reactivity. In addition, you will notice two trends the first ionization enthalpy generally increases as we go across a period and decreases as we descend in a group. The ionization enthalpy and atomic radius are closely related properties.


The second ionization enthalpy will be higher than the first ionization enthalpy because

options.text: it is more difficult to remove an electron from a negatively charged ion
options.html: it is more difficult to remove an electron from a negatively charged ion

options.text: it is more difficult to remove an electron from a neutrally charged ion
options.html: it is more difficult to remove an electron from a neutrally charged ion

options.text: it is more difficult to remove an electron from a positively charged ion
options.html: it is more difficult to remove an electron from a positively charged ion

options.text: it's not difficult to remove an electron from a neutrally charged ion
options.html: it's not difficult to remove an electron from a neutrally charged ion

correct_options: it is more difficult to remove an electron from a positively charged ion
questions.explanation: it is more difficult to remove an electron from a positively charged ion

A quantitative measure of the tendency of an element to lose electrons is given by its Ionization Enthalpy. The second ionization enthalpy will be higher than the first ionization enthalpy.
